36: ## Installation
37: 
38: Install the gem and add to the application's Gemfile by executing:
39: 
40:     bundle add langchainrb
41: 
42: If bundler is not being used to manage dependencies, install the gem by executing:
43: 
44:     gem install langchainrb
45: 
46: Additional gems may be required. They're not included by default so you can include only what you need.
47: 
48: ## Usage
49: 
50: ```ruby
51: require "langchain"
52: ```
53: 
54: # Unified Interface for LLMs
55: 
56: The `Langchain::LLM` module provides a unified interface for interacting with various Large Language Model (LLM) providers. This abstraction allows you to easily switch between different LLM backends without changing your application code.
57: 
58: ## Supported LLM Providers
59: 
60: - Anthropic
61: - AWS Bedrock
62: - Azure OpenAI
63: - Cohere
64: - Google Gemini
65: - Google Vertex AI
66: - HuggingFace
67: - Mistral AI
68: - Ollama
69: - OpenAI
70: - Replicate
71: 
72: ## Usage
73: 
74: All LLM classes inherit from `Langchain::LLM::Base` and provide a consistent interface for common operations:
75: 
76: 1. Generating embeddings
77: 2. Generating prompt completions
78: 3. Generating chat completions
79: 
80: ### Initialization
81: 
82: Most LLM classes can be initialized with an API key and optional default options:
83: 
84: ```ruby
85: llm = Langchain::LLM::OpenAI.new(
86:   api_key: ENV["OPENAI_API_KEY"],
87:   default_options: { temperature: 0.7, chat_model: "gpt-4o" }
88: )
89: ```
90: 
91: ### Generating Embeddings
92: 
93: Use the `embed` method to generate embeddings for given text:
94: 
95: ```ruby
96: response = llm.embed(text: "Hello, world!")
97: embedding = response.embedding
98: ```
99: 
100: #### Accepted parameters for `embed()`
101: 
102: - `text`: (Required) The input text to embed.
103: - `model`: (Optional) The model name to use or default embedding model will be used.
104: 
105: ### Prompt completions
106: 
107: Use the `complete` method to generate completions for a given prompt:
108: 
109: ```ruby
110: response = llm.complete(prompt: "Once upon a time")
111: completion = response.completion
112: ```
113: 
114: #### Accepted parameters for `complete()`
115: 
116: - `prompt`: (Required) The input prompt for completion.
117: - `max_tokens`: (Optional) The maximum number of tokens to generate.
118: - `temperature`: (Optional) Controls randomness in generation. Higher values (e.g., 0.8) make output more random, while lower values (e.g., 0.2) make it more deterministic.
119: - `top_p`: (Optional) An alternative to temperature, controls diversity of generated tokens.
120: - `n`: (Optional) Number of completions to generate for each prompt.
121: - `stop`: (Optional) Sequences where the API will stop generating further tokens.
122: - `presence_penalty`: (Optional) Penalizes new tokens based on their presence in the text so far.
123: - `frequency_penalty`: (Optional) Penalizes new tokens based on their frequency in the text so far.
124: 
125: ### Generating Chat Completions
126: 
127: Use the `chat` method to generate chat completions:
128: 
129: ```ruby
130: messages = [
131:   { role: "system", content: "You are a helpful assistant." },
132:   { role: "user", content: "What's the weather like today?" }
133:   # Google Gemini and Google VertexAI expect messages in a different format:
134:   # { role: "user", parts: [{ text: "why is the sky blue?" }]}
135: ]
136: response = llm.chat(messages: messages)
137: chat_completion = response.chat_completion
138: ```
139: 
140: #### Accepted parameters for `chat()`
141: 
142: - `messages`: (Required) An array of message objects representing the conversation history.
143: - `model`: (Optional) The specific chat model to use.
144: - `temperature`: (Optional) Controls randomness in generation.
145: - `top_p`: (Optional) An alternative to temperature, controls diversity of generated tokens.
146: - `n`: (Optional) Number of chat completion choices to generate.
147: - `max_tokens`: (Optional) The maximum number of tokens to generate in the chat completion.
148: - `stop`: (Optional) Sequences where the API will stop generating further tokens.
149: - `presence_penalty`: (Optional) Penalizes new tokens based on their presence in the text so far.
150: - `frequency_penalty`: (Optional) Penalizes new tokens based on their frequency in the text so far.
151: - `logit_bias`: (Optional) Modifies the likelihood of specified tokens appearing in the completion.
152: - `user`: (Optional) A unique identifier representing your end-user.
153: - `tools`: (Optional) A list of tools the model may call.
154: - `tool_choice`: (Optional) Controls how the model calls functions.
155: 
156: ## Switching LLM Providers
157: 
158: Thanks to the unified interface, you can easily switch between different LLM providers by changing the class you instantiate:
159: 
160: ```ruby
161: # Using Anthropic
162: anthropic_llm = Langchain::LLM::Anthropic.new(api_key: ENV["ANTHROPIC_API_KEY"])
163: 
164: # Using Google Gemini
165: gemini_llm = Langchain::LLM::GoogleGemini.new(api_key: ENV["GOOGLE_GEMINI_API_KEY"])
166: 
167: # Using OpenAI
168: openai_llm = Langchain::LLM::OpenAI.new(api_key: ENV["OPENAI_API_KEY"])
169: ```
170: 
171: ## Response Objects
172: 
173: Each LLM method returns a response object that provides a consistent interface for accessing the results:
174: 
175: - `embedding`: Returns the embedding vector
176: - `completion`: Returns the generated text completion
177: - `chat_completion`: Returns the generated chat completion
178: - `tool_calls`: Returns tool calls made by the LLM
179: - `prompt_tokens`: Returns the number of tokens in the prompt
180: - `completion_tokens`: Returns the number of tokens in the completion
181: - `total_tokens`: Returns the total number of tokens used
182: 
183: > [!NOTE]
184: > While the core interface is consistent across providers, some LLMs may offer additional features or parameters. Consult the documentation for each LLM class to learn about provider-specific capabilities and options.
185: 
186: ### Prompt Management
187: 
188: #### Prompt Templates
189: 
190: Create a prompt with input variables:
191: 
192: ```ruby
193: prompt = Langchain::Prompt::PromptTemplate.new(template: "Tell me a {adjective} joke about {content}.", input_variables: ["adjective", "content"])
194: prompt.format(adjective: "funny", content: "chickens") # "Tell me a funny joke about chickens."
195: ```
196: 
197: Creating a PromptTemplate using just a prompt and no input_variables:
198: 
199: ```ruby
200: prompt = Langchain::Prompt::PromptTemplate.from_template("Tell me a funny joke about chickens.")
201: prompt.input_variables # []
202: prompt.format # "Tell me a funny joke about chickens."
203: ```
204: 
205: Save prompt template to JSON file:
206: 
207: ```ruby
208: prompt.save(file_path: "spec/fixtures/prompt/prompt_template.json")
209: ```
210: 
211: Loading a new prompt template using a JSON file:
212: 
213: ```ruby
214: prompt = Langchain::Prompt.load_from_path(file_path: "spec/fixtures/prompt/prompt_template.json")
215: prompt.input_variables # ["adjective", "content"]
216: ```
217: 
218: #### Few Shot Prompt Templates
219: 
220: Create a prompt with a few shot examples:
221: 
222: ```ruby
223: prompt = Langchain::Prompt::FewShotPromptTemplate.new(
224:   prefix: "Write antonyms for the following words.",
225:   suffix: "Input: {adjective}\nOutput:",
226:   example_prompt: Langchain::Prompt::PromptTemplate.new(
227:     input_variables: ["input", "output"],
228:     template: "Input: {input}\nOutput: {output}"
229:   ),
230:   examples: [
231:     { "input": "happy", "output": "sad" },
232:     { "input": "tall", "output": "short" }
233:   ],
234:    input_variables: ["adjective"]
235: )
236: 
237: prompt.format(adjective: "good")
238: 
239: # Write antonyms for the following words.
240: #
241: # Input: happy
242: # Output: sad
243: #
244: # Input: tall
245: # Output: short
246: #
247: # Input: good
248: # Output:
249: ```
250: 
251: Save prompt template to JSON file:
252: 
253: ```ruby
254: prompt.save(file_path: "spec/fixtures/prompt/few_shot_prompt_template.json")
255: ```
256: 
257: Loading a new prompt template using a JSON file:
258: 
259: ```ruby
260: prompt = Langchain::Prompt.load_from_path(file_path: "spec/fixtures/prompt/few_shot_prompt_template.json")
261: prompt.prefix # "Write antonyms for the following words."
262: ```
263: 
264: Loading a new prompt template using a YAML file:
265: 
266: ```ruby
267: prompt = Langchain::Prompt.load_from_path(file_path: "spec/fixtures/prompt/prompt_template.yaml")
268: prompt.input_variables #=> ["adjective", "content"]
269: ```
270: 
271: 
272: ### Output Parsers
273: 
274: Parse LLM text responses into structured output, such as JSON.
275: 
276: #### Structured Output Parser
277: 
278: You can use the `StructuredOutputParser` to generate a prompt that instructs the LLM to provide a JSON response adhering to a specific JSON schema:
279: 
280: ```ruby
281: json_schema = {
282:   type: "object",
283:   properties: {
284:     name: {
285:       type: "string",
286:       description: "Persons name"
287:     },
288:     age: {
289:       type: "number",
290:       description: "Persons age"
291:     },
292:     interests: {
293:       type: "array",
294:       items: {
295:         type: "object",
296:         properties: {
297:           interest: {
298:             type: "string",
299:             description: "A topic of interest"
300:           },
301:           levelOfInterest: {
302:             type: "number",
303:             description: "A value between 0 and 100 of how interested the person is in this interest"
304:           }
305:         },
306:         required: ["interest", "levelOfInterest"],
307:         additionalProperties: false
308:       },
309:       minItems: 1,
310:       maxItems: 3,
311:       description: "A list of the person's interests"
312:     }
313:   },
314:   required: ["name", "age", "interests"],
315:   additionalProperties: false
316: }
317: parser = Langchain::OutputParsers::StructuredOutputParser.from_json_schema(json_schema)
318: prompt = Langchain::Prompt::PromptTemplate.new(template: "Generate details of a fictional character.\n{format_instructions}\nCharacter description: {description}", input_variables: ["description", "format_instructions"])
319: prompt_text = prompt.format(description: "Korean chemistry student", format_instructions: parser.get_format_instructions)
320: # Generate details of a fictional character.
321: # You must format your output as a JSON value that adheres to a given "JSON Schema" instance.
322: # ...
323: ```
324: 
325: Then parse the llm response:
326: 
327: ```ruby
328: llm = Langchain::LLM::OpenAI.new(api_key: ENV["OPENAI_API_KEY"])
329: llm_response = llm.chat(messages: [{role: "user", content: prompt_text}]).completion
330: parser.parse(llm_response)
331: # {
332: #   "name" => "Kim Ji-hyun",
333: #   "age" => 22,
334: #   "interests" => [
335: #     {
336: #       "interest" => "Organic Chemistry",
337: #       "levelOfInterest" => 85
338: #     },
339: #     ...
340: #   ]
341: # }
342: ```
343: 
344: If the parser fails to parse the LLM response, you can use the `OutputFixingParser`. It sends an error message, prior output, and the original prompt text to the LLM, asking for a "fixed" response:
345: 
346: ```ruby
347: begin
348:   parser.parse(llm_response)
349: rescue Langchain::OutputParsers::OutputParserException => e
350:   fix_parser = Langchain::OutputParsers::OutputFixingParser.from_llm(
351:     llm: llm,
352:     parser: parser
353:   )
354:   fix_parser.parse(llm_response)
355: end
356: ```
357: 
358: Alternatively, if you don't need to handle the `OutputParserException`, you can simplify the code:
359: 
360: ```ruby
361: # we already have the `OutputFixingParser`:
362: # parser = Langchain::OutputParsers::StructuredOutputParser.from_json_schema(json_schema)
363: fix_parser = Langchain::OutputParsers::OutputFixingParser.from_llm(
364:   llm: llm,
365:   parser: parser
366: )
367: fix_parser.parse(llm_response)
368: ```
369: 
370: See [here](https://github.com/patterns-ai-core/langchainrb/tree/main/examples/create_and_manage_prompt_templates_using_structured_output_parser.rb) for a concrete example
371: 
372: ## Building Retrieval Augment Generation (RAG) system
373: RAG is a methodology that assists LLMs generate accurate and up-to-date information.
374: A typical RAG workflow follows the 3 steps below:
375: 1. Relevant knowledge (or data) is retrieved from the knowledge base (typically a vector search DB)
376: 2. A prompt, containing retrieved knowledge above, is constructed.
377: 3. LLM receives the prompt above to generate a text completion.
378: Most common use-case for a RAG system is powering Q&A systems where users pose natural language questions and receive answers in natural language.
379: 
380: ### Vector search databases
381: Langchain.rb provides a convenient unified interface on top of supported vectorsearch databases that make it easy to configure your index, add data, query and retrieve from it.
382: 
383: #### Supported vector search databases and features:
384: 
385: | Database                                                                                   | Open-source        | Cloud offering     |
386: | --------                                                                                   |:------------------:| :------------:     |
387: | [Chroma](https://trychroma.com/?utm_source=langchainrb&utm_medium=github)                  | ✅                 | ✅                 |
388: | [Hnswlib](https://github.com/nmslib/hnswlib/?utm_source=langchainrb&utm_medium=github)     | ✅                 | ❌                 |
389: | [Milvus](https://milvus.io/?utm_source=langchainrb&utm_medium=github)                      | ✅                 | ✅ Zilliz Cloud    |
390: | [Pinecone](https://www.pinecone.io/?utm_source=langchainrb&utm_medium=github)              | ❌                 | ✅                 |
391: | [Pgvector](https://github.com/pgvector/pgvector/?utm_source=langchainrb&utm_medium=github) | ✅                 | ✅                 |
392: | [Qdrant](https://qdrant.tech/?utm_source=langchainrb&utm_medium=github)                    | ✅                 | ✅                 |
393: | [Weaviate](https://weaviate.io/?utm_source=langchainrb&utm_medium=github)                  | ✅                 | ✅                 |
394: | [Elasticsearch](https://www.elastic.co/?utm_source=langchainrb&utm_medium=github)          | ✅                 | ✅                 |
395: 
396: ### Using Vector Search Databases 🔍
397: 
398: Pick the vector search database you'll be using, add the gem dependency and instantiate the client:
399: ```ruby
400: gem "weaviate-ruby", "~> 0.8.9"
401: ```
402: 
403: Choose and instantiate the LLM provider you'll be using to generate embeddings
404: ```ruby
405: llm = Langchain::LLM::OpenAI.new(api_key: ENV["OPENAI_API_KEY"])
406: ```
407: 
408: ```ruby
409: client = Langchain::Vectorsearch::Weaviate.new(
410:     url: ENV["WEAVIATE_URL"],
411:     api_key: ENV["WEAVIATE_API_KEY"],
412:     index_name: "Documents",
413:     llm: llm
414: )
415: ```
416: 
417: You can instantiate any other supported vector search database:
418: ```ruby
419: client = Langchain::Vectorsearch::Chroma.new(...)   # `gem "chroma-db", "~> 0.6.0"`
420: client = Langchain::Vectorsearch::Hnswlib.new(...)  # `gem "hnswlib", "~> 0.8.1"`
421: client = Langchain::Vectorsearch::Milvus.new(...)   # `gem "milvus", "~> 0.9.3"`
422: client = Langchain::Vectorsearch::Pinecone.new(...) # `gem "pinecone", "~> 1.0"`
423: client = Langchain::Vectorsearch::Pgvector.new(...) # `gem "pgvector", "~> 0.2"`
424: client = Langchain::Vectorsearch::Qdrant.new(...)   # `gem "qdrant-ruby", "~> 0.9.3"`
425: client = Langchain::Vectorsearch::Elasticsearch.new(...)   # `gem "elasticsearch", "~> 8.2.0"`
426: ```
427: 
428: Create the default schema:
429: ```ruby
430: client.create_default_schema
431: ```
432: 
433: Add plain text data to your vector search database:
434: ```ruby
435: client.add_texts(
436:   texts: [
437:     "Begin by preheating your oven to 375°F (190°C). Prepare four boneless, skinless chicken breasts by cutting a pocket into the side of each breast, being careful not to cut all the way through. Season the chicken with salt and pepper to taste. In a large skillet, melt 2 tablespoons of unsalted butter over medium heat. Add 1 small diced onion and 2 minced garlic cloves, and cook until softened, about 3-4 minutes. Add 8 ounces of fresh spinach and cook until wilted, about 3 minutes. Remove the skillet from heat and let the mixture cool slightly.",
438:       "In a bowl, combine the spinach mixture with 4 ounces of softened cream cheese, 1/4 cup of grated Parmesan cheese, 1/4 cup of shredded mozzarella cheese, and 1/4 teaspoon of red pepper flakes. Mix until well combined. Stuff each chicken breast pocket with an equal amount of the spinach mixture. Seal the pocket with a toothpick if necessary. In the same skillet, heat 1 tablespoon of olive oil over medium-high heat. Add the stuffed chicken breasts and sear on each side for 3-4 minutes, or until golden brown."
439:   ]
440: )
441: ```
442: 
443: Or use the file parsers to load, parse and index data into your database:
444: ```ruby
445: my_pdf = Langchain.root.join("path/to/my.pdf")
446: my_text = Langchain.root.join("path/to/my.txt")
447: my_docx = Langchain.root.join("path/to/my.docx")
448: 
449: client.add_data(paths: [my_pdf, my_text, my_docx])
450: ```
451: Supported file formats: docx, html, pdf, text, json, jsonl, csv, xlsx, eml, pptx.
452: 
453: Retrieve similar documents based on the query string passed in:
454: ```ruby
455: client.similarity_search(
456:   query:,
457:   k:       # number of results to be retrieved
458: )
459: ```
460: 
461: Retrieve similar documents based on the query string passed in via the [HyDE technique](https://arxiv.org/abs/2212.10496):
462: ```ruby
463: client.similarity_search_with_hyde()
464: ```
465: 
466: Retrieve similar documents based on the embedding passed in:
467: ```ruby
468: client.similarity_search_by_vector(
469:   embedding:,
470:   k:       # number of results to be retrieved
471: )
472: ```
473: 
474: RAG-based querying
475: ```ruby
476: client.ask(question: "...")
477: ```

479: ## Assistants
480: `Langchain::Assistant` is a powerful and flexible class that combines Large Language Models (LLMs), tools, and conversation management to create intelligent, interactive assistants. It's designed to handle complex conversations, execute tools, and provide coherent responses based on the context of the interaction.
481: 
482: ### Features
483: * Supports multiple LLM providers (OpenAI, Google Gemini, Anthropic, Mistral AI and open-source models via Ollama)
484: * Integrates with various tools to extend functionality
485: * Manages conversation threads
486: * Handles automatic and manual tool execution
487: * Supports different message formats for various LLM providers
488: 
489: ### Usage
490: ```ruby
491: llm = Langchain::LLM::OpenAI.new(api_key: ENV["OPENAI_API_KEY"])
492: assistant = Langchain::Assistant.new(
493:   llm: llm,
494:   instructions: "You're a helpful AI assistant",
495:   tools: [Langchain::Tool::NewsRetriever.new(api_key: ENV["NEWS_API_KEY"])]
496: )
497: 
498: # Add a user message and run the assistant
499: assistant.add_message_and_run!(content: "What's the latest news about AI?")
500: 
501: # Supply an image to the assistant
502: assistant.add_message_and_run!(
503:   content: "Describe this image.",
504:   image_url: "https://upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/d/dd/Gfp-wisconsin-madison-the-nature-boardwalk.jpg/2560px-Gfp-wisconsin-madison-the-nature-boardwalk.jpg"
505: )
506: 
507: # Access the conversation thread
508: messages = assistant.messages
509: 
510: # Run the assistant with automatic tool execution
511: assistant.run(auto_tool_execution: true)
512: 
513: # If you want to stream the response, you can add a response handler
514: assistant = Langchain::Assistant.new(
515:   llm: llm,
516:   instructions: "You're a helpful AI assistant",
517:   tools: [Langchain::Tool::NewsRetriever.new(api_key: ENV["NEWS_API_KEY"])]
518: ) do |response_chunk|
519:   # ...handle the response stream
520:   # print(response_chunk.inspect)
521: end
522: assistant.add_message(content: "Hello")
523: assistant.run(auto_tool_execution: true)
524: ```
525: 
526: Note that streaming is not currently supported for all LLMs.
527: 
528: ### Configuration
529: * `llm`: The LLM instance to use (required)
530: * `tools`: An array of tool instances (optional)
531: * `instructions`: System instructions for the assistant (optional)
532: * `tool_choice`: Specifies how tools should be selected. Default: "auto". A specific tool function name can be passed. This will force the Assistant to **always** use this function.
533: * `parallel_tool_calls`: Whether to make multiple parallel tool calls. Default: true
534: * `add_message_callback`: A callback function (proc, lambda) that is called when any message is added to the conversation (optional)
535: ```ruby
536: assistant.add_message_callback = -> (message) { puts "New message: #{message}" }
537: ```
538: * `tool_execution_callback`: A callback function (proc, lambda) that is called right before a tool is executed (optional)
539: ```ruby
540: assistant.tool_execution_callback = -> (tool_call_id, tool_name, method_name, tool_arguments) { puts "Executing tool_call_id: #{tool_call_id}, tool_name: #{tool_name}, method_name: #{method_name}, tool_arguments: #{tool_arguments}" }
541: ```
542: 
543: ### Key Methods
544: * `add_message`: Adds a user message to the messages array
545: * `run!`: Processes the conversation and generates responses
546: * `add_message_and_run!`: Combines adding a message and running the assistant
547: * `submit_tool_output`: Manually submit output to a tool call
548: * `messages`: Returns a list of ongoing messages
549: 
550: ### Built-in Tools 🛠️
551: * `Langchain::Tool::Calculator`: Useful for evaluating math expressions. Requires `gem "eqn"`.
552: * `Langchain::Tool::Database`: Connect your SQL database. Requires `gem "sequel"`.
553: * `Langchain::Tool::FileSystem`: Interact with the file system (read & write).
554: * `Langchain::Tool::GoogleSearch`: Wrapper around SerpApi's Google Search API. Requires `gem "google_search_results"`.
555: * `Langchain::Tool::NewsRetriever`: A wrapper around [NewsApi.org](https://newsapi.org) to fetch news articles.
556: * `Langchain::Tool::RubyCodeInterpreter`: Useful for evaluating generated Ruby code. Requires `gem "safe_ruby"` (In need of a better solution).
557: * `Langchain::Tool::Tavily`: A wrapper around [Tavily AI](https://tavily.com).
558: * `Langchain::Tool::Vectorsearch`: A wrapper for vector search classes.
559: * `Langchain::Tool::Weather`: Calls [Open Weather API](https://home.openweathermap.org) to retrieve the current weather.
560: * `Langchain::Tool::Wikipedia`: Calls Wikipedia API. Requires `gem "wikipedia-client"`.
561: 
562: ### Creating custom Tools
563: The Langchain::Assistant can be easily extended with custom tools by creating classes that `extend Langchain::ToolDefinition` module and implement required methods.
564: ```ruby
565: class MovieInfoTool
566:   extend Langchain::ToolDefinition
567: 
568:   define_function :search_movie, description: "MovieInfoTool: Search for a movie by title" do
569:     property :query, type: "string", description: "The movie title to search for", required: true
570:   end
571: 
572:   define_function :get_movie_details, description: "MovieInfoTool: Get detailed information about a specific movie" do
573:     property :movie_id, type: "integer", description: "The TMDb ID of the movie", required: true
574:   end
575: 
576:   def initialize(api_key:)
577:     @api_key = api_key
578:   end
579: 
580:   def search_movie(query:)
581:     tool_response(...)
582:   end
583: 
584:   def get_movie_details(movie_id:)
585:     tool_response(...)
586:   end
587: end
588: ```
589: 
590: #### Example usage:
591: ```ruby
592: movie_tool = MovieInfoTool.new(api_key: "...")
593: 
594: assistant = Langchain::Assistant.new(
595:   llm: llm,
596:   instructions: "You're a helpful AI assistant that can provide movie information",
597:   tools: [movie_tool]
598: )
599: 
600: assistant.add_message_and_run(content: "Can you tell me about the movie 'Inception'?")
601: # Check the response in the last message in the conversation
602: assistant.messages.last
603: ```
604: 
605: ### Error Handling
606: The assistant includes error handling for invalid inputs, unsupported LLM types, and tool execution failures. It uses a state machine to manage the conversation flow and handle different scenarios gracefully.
607: 
608: ### Demos
609: 1. [Building an AI Assistant that operates a simulated E-commerce Store](https://www.loom.com/share/83aa4fd8dccb492aad4ca95da40ed0b2)
610: 2. [New Langchain.rb Assistants interface](https://www.loom.com/share/e883a4a49b8746c1b0acf9d58cf6da36)
611: 3. [Langchain.rb Assistant demo with NewsRetriever and function calling on Gemini](https://youtu.be/-ieyahrpDpM&t=1477s) - [code](https://github.com/palladius/gemini-news-crawler)
612: 
613: ## Evaluations (Evals)
614: The Evaluations module is a collection of tools that can be used to evaluate and track the performance of the output products by LLM and your RAG (Retrieval Augmented Generation) pipelines.
615: 
616: ### RAGAS
617: Ragas helps you evaluate your Retrieval Augmented Generation (RAG) pipelines. The implementation is based on this [paper](https://arxiv.org/abs/2309.15217) and the original Python [repo](https://github.com/explodinggradients/ragas). Ragas tracks the following 3 metrics and assigns the 0.0 - 1.0 scores:
618: * Faithfulness - the answer is grounded in the given context.
619: * Context Relevance - the retrieved context is focused, containing little to no irrelevant information.
620: * Answer Relevance - the generated answer addresses the actual question that was provided.
621: 
622: ```ruby
623: # We recommend using Langchain::LLM::OpenAI as your llm for Ragas
624: ragas = Langchain::Evals::Ragas::Main.new(llm: llm)
625: 
626: # The answer that the LLM generated
627: # The question (or the original prompt) that was asked
628: # The context that was retrieved (usually from a vectorsearch database)
629: ragas.score(answer: "", question: "", context: "")
630: # =>
631: # {
632: #   ragas_score: 0.6601257446503674,
633: #   answer_relevance_score: 0.9573145866787608,
634: #   context_relevance_score: 0.6666666666666666,
635: #   faithfulness_score: 0.5
636: # }
637: ```
638: 
639: ## Examples
640: Additional examples available: [/examples](https://github.com/patterns-ai-core/langchainrb/tree/main/examples)
641: 
642: ## Logging
643: 
644: Langchain.rb uses the standard Ruby [Logger](https://ruby-doc.org/stdlib-2.4.0/libdoc/logger/rdoc/Logger.html) mechanism and defaults to same `level` value (currently `Logger::DEBUG`).
645: 
646: To show all log messages:
647: 
648: ```ruby
649: Langchain.logger.level = Logger::DEBUG
650: ```
651: 
652: The logger logs to `STDOUT` by default. In order to configure the log destination (ie. log to a file) do:
653: 
654: ```ruby
655: Langchain.logger = Logger.new("path/to/file", **Langchain::LOGGER_OPTIONS)
656: ```
657: 
658: ## Problems
659: If you're having issues installing `unicode` gem required by `pragmatic_segmenter`, try running:
660: ```bash
661: gem install unicode -- --with-cflags="-Wno-incompatible-function-pointer-types"
662: ```
663: 
664: ## Development
665: 
666: 1. `git clone https://github.com/patterns-ai-core/langchainrb.git`
667: 2. `cp .env.example .env`, then fill out the environment variables in `.env`
668: 3. `bundle exec rake` to ensure that the tests pass and to run standardrb
669: 4. `bin/console` to load the gem in a REPL session. Feel free to add your own instances of LLMs, Tools, Agents, etc. and experiment with them.
670: 5. Optionally, install lefthook git hooks for pre-commit to auto lint: `gem install lefthook && lefthook install -f`
671:

1: # frozen_string_literal: true

3: module Langchain::LLM
4:   #
5:   # Wrapper around Anthropic APIs.
6:   #
7:   # Gem requirements:
8:   #   gem "anthropic", "~> 1.10.0"
9:   #
10:   # Usage:
11:   #     llm = Langchain::LLM::Anthropic.new(api_key: ENV["ANTHROPIC_API_KEY"])
12:   #
13:   class Anthropic < Base
14:     DEFAULTS = {
15:       temperature: 0.0,
16:       completion_model: "claude-2.1",
17:       chat_model: "claude-sonnet-4-6",
18:       max_tokens: 256
19:     }.freeze
20: 
21:     # Initialize an Anthropic LLM instance
22:     #
23:     # @param api_key [String] The API key to use
24:     # @param llm_options [Hash] Options to pass to the Anthropic client
25:     # @param default_options [Hash] Default options to use on every call to LLM, e.g.: { temperature:, completion_model:, chat_model:, max_tokens:, thinking: }
26:     # @return [Langchain::LLM::Anthropic] Langchain::LLM::Anthropic instance
27:     def initialize(api_key:, llm_options: {}, default_options: {})
28:       depends_on "anthropic"
29: 
30:       @client = ::Anthropic::Client.new(api_key: api_key, **llm_options)
31:       @defaults = DEFAULTS.merge(default_options)
32:       chat_parameters.update(
33:         model: {default: @defaults[:chat_model]},
34:         temperature: {default: @defaults[:temperature]},
35:         max_tokens: {default: @defaults[:max_tokens]},
36:         metadata: {},
37:         system: {},
38:         thinking: {default: @defaults[:thinking]},
39:         request_options: {}
40:       )
41:       chat_parameters.ignore(:n, :user)
42:       chat_parameters.remap(stop: :stop_sequences)
43:     end
44: 
45:     # Generate a completion for a given prompt
46:     #
47:     # @param prompt [String] Prompt to generate a completion for
48:     # @param model [String] The model to use
49:     # @param max_tokens_to_sample [Integer] The maximum number of tokens to sample
50:     # @param stop_sequences [Array<String>] The stop sequences to use
51:     # @param temperature [Float] The temperature to use
52:     # @param top_p [Float] The top p value to use
53:     # @param top_k [Integer] The top k value to use
54:     # @param metadata [Hash] The metadata to use
55:     # @param stream [Boolean] Whether to stream the response
56:     # @return [Langchain::LLM::Response::AnthropicResponse] The completion
57:     def complete(
58:       prompt:,
59:       model: @defaults[:completion_model],
60:       max_tokens: @defaults[:max_tokens],
61:       stop_sequences: nil,
62:       temperature: @defaults[:temperature],
63:       top_p: nil,
64:       top_k: nil,
65:       metadata: nil,
66:       stream: nil
67:     )
68:       raise ArgumentError.new("model argument is required") if model.empty?
69:       raise ArgumentError.new("max_tokens argument is required") if max_tokens.nil?
70: 
71:       parameters = {
72:         model: model,
73:         prompt: prompt,
74:         max_tokens_to_sample: max_tokens,
75:         temperature: temperature
76:       }
77:       parameters[:stop_sequences] = stop_sequences if stop_sequences
78:       parameters[:top_p] = top_p if top_p
79:       parameters[:top_k] = top_k if top_k
80:       parameters[:metadata] = metadata if metadata
81:       parameters[:stream] = stream if stream
82: 
83:       response = with_api_error_handling do
84:         client.complete(parameters: parameters)
85:       end
86: 
87:       Langchain::LLM::Response::AnthropicResponse.new(response)
88:     end
89: 
90:     # Generate a chat completion for given messages
91:     #
92:     # @param [Hash] params unified chat parmeters from [Langchain::LLM::Parameters::Chat::SCHEMA]
93:     # @option params [Array<String>] :messages Input messages
94:     # @option params [String] :model The model that will complete your prompt
95:     # @option params [Integer] :max_tokens Maximum number of tokens to generate before stopping
96:     # @option params [Hash] :metadata Object describing metadata about the request
97:     # @option params [Array<String>] :stop_sequences Custom text sequences that will cause the model to stop generating
98:     # @option params [Boolean] :stream Whether to incrementally stream the response using server-sent events
99:     # @option params [String] :system System prompt
100:     # @option params [Float] :temperature Amount of randomness injected into the response
101:     # @option params [Array<String>] :tools Definitions of tools that the model may use
102:     # @option params [Hash] :thinking Enable extended thinking mode, e.g. { type: "enabled", budget_tokens: 4000 }
103:     # @option params [Integer] :top_k Only sample from the top K options for each subsequent token
104:     # @option params [Float] :top_p Use nucleus sampling.
105:     # @return [Langchain::LLM::Response::AnthropicResponse] The chat completion
106:     def chat(params = {}, &block)
107:       parameters = chat_parameters.to_params(params)
108: 
109:       raise ArgumentError.new("messages argument is required") if Array(parameters[:messages]).empty?
110:       raise ArgumentError.new("model argument is required") if parameters[:model].empty?
111:       raise ArgumentError.new("max_tokens argument is required") if parameters[:max_tokens].nil?
112: 
113:       if block
114:         @response_chunks = []
115:         parameters[:stream] = proc do |chunk|
116:           @response_chunks << chunk
117:           yield chunk
118:         end
119:       end
120: 
121:       response = client.messages.create(parameters)
122: 
123:       response = response_from_chunks if block
124:       reset_response_chunks
125: 
126:       Langchain::LLM::Response::AnthropicResponse.new(response)
127:     end
128: 
129:     def with_api_error_handling
130:       response = yield
131:       return if response.empty?
132: 
133:       raise Langchain::LLM::ApiError.new "Anthropic API error: #{response.dig("error", "message")}" if response&.dig("error")
134: 
135:       response
136:     end
137: 
138:     def response_from_chunks
139:       grouped_chunks = @response_chunks.group_by { |chunk| chunk["index"] }.except(nil)
140: 
141:       usage_chunk = @response_chunks.find { |chunk| chunk["type"] == "message_delta" }
142:       usage = usage_chunk&.dig("usage")&.transform_keys(&:to_sym)
143:       stop_reason = usage_chunk&.dig("delta", "stop_reason")
144: 
145:       content = grouped_chunks.map do |_index, chunks|
146:         text = chunks.map { |chunk| chunk.dig("delta", "text") }.join
147:         if !text.nil? && !text.empty?
148:           {type: "text", text: text}
149:         else
150:           tool_calls_from_choice_chunks(chunks)
151:         end
152:       end.flatten
153: 
154:       first_chunk = @response_chunks.first
155:       {
156:         id: first_chunk&.dig("id") || first_chunk&.dig("message", "id"),
157:         model: first_chunk&.dig("model") || first_chunk&.dig("message", "model"),
158:         content: content,
159:         usage: usage,
160:         role: "assistant",
161:         stop_reason: stop_reason
162:       }
163:     end
164: 
165:     def tool_calls_from_choice_chunks(chunks)
166:       return unless (first_block = chunks.find { |chunk| chunk.dig("content_block", "type") == "tool_use" })
167: 
168:       chunks.group_by { |chunk| chunk["index"] }.map do |index, chunks|
169:         input = chunks.select { |chunk| chunk.dig("delta", "partial_json") }
170:           .map! { |chunk| chunk.dig("delta", "partial_json") }.join
171:         {
172:           id: first_block.dig("content_block", "id"),
173:           type: "tool_use",
174:           name: first_block.dig("content_block", "name"),
175:           input: input.empty? ? nil : JSON.parse(input).transform_keys(&:to_sym)
176:         }
177:       end.compact
178:     end
179: 
180:     private
181: 
182:     def reset_response_chunks
183:       @response_chunks = []
184:     end
185:   end
186: end

**new_provider_adapter.rb:**
```ruby
module Langchain::Assistant::LLM::Adapters
  class NewProvider < Base
    def build_chat_params(messages:, instructions:, tools:, tool_choice:, parallel_tool_calls:)
      params = {messages: messages}
      if tools.any?
        params[:tools] = build_tools(tools)
        params[:tool_choice] = build_tool_choice(tool_choice)
      end
      params
    end
    
    def extract_tool_call_args(tool_call:)
      tool_call_id = tool_call.dig("id")
      function_name = tool_call.dig("function", "name")
      tool_name, method_name = function_name.split("__")
      tool_arguments = tool_call.dig("function", "arguments")
      tool_arguments = JSON.parse(tool_arguments, symbolize_names: true) unless tool_arguments.is_a?(Hash)
      [tool_call_id, tool_name, method_name, tool_arguments]
    end
    
    def build_message(role:, content: nil, image_url: nil, tool_calls: [], tool_call_id: nil)
      Messages::NewProviderMessage.new(role: role, content: content, image_url: image_url, tool_calls: tool_calls, tool_call_id: tool_call_id)
    end
    
    def support_system_message?
      Messages::NewProviderMessage::ROLES.include?("system")
    end
    
    def tool_role
      Messages::NewProviderMessage::TOOL_ROLE
    end
    
    def allowed_tool_choices
      ["auto", "none"]
    end
    
    def available_tool_names(tools)
      build_tools(tools).map { |tool| tool.dig(:function, :name) }
    end
    
    private
    
    def build_tools(tools)
      tools.map { |tool| tool.class.function_schemas.to_openai_format }.flatten
    end
    
    def build_tool_choice(choice)
      choice # or {"type" => "function", "function" => {"name" => choice}} if not "auto"
    end
  end
end
